title: Medical scene safety and first helper role
category: Medical and first · Aid · Disaster · Health
summary: Medical-heavy survival entry for a safe first response before touching an injured person. It is designed for offline emergencies and does not replace professional care.
content: Use this entry to organize a safe first response before touching an injured person. Start with scene safety, hand hygiene, calm communication, and written timing. Keep the person comfortable, protect privacy, avoid risky experiments, and prepare a clear handover for a doctor, ambulance, clinic, pharmacist, or trained first-aider. Watch especially for: fire, traffic, electricity, gas smell, violence, unstable buildings, or more casualties. In a long emergency, review the person regularly instead of assuming they are stable.
materials: clean water if available. Context: “Medical scene safety and first helper role”.
soap or sanitizer if available — for «Medical scene safety and first helper role»
gloves or clean barrier if available — for «Medical scene safety and first helper role»
paper and pen. Context: “Medical scene safety and first helper role”.
phone or radio if available — for «Medical scene safety and first helper role»
labels for times and medicines — for «Medical scene safety and first helper role»
steps: Check that the place is safe for the helper and the injured or sick person. Apply it specifically within the “Medical scene safety and first helper role” procedure.
Call emergency help or send a reliable person for help when any red flag is present. Apply it specifically within the “Medical scene safety and first helper role” procedure.
Write the time the problem started, what changed, medicines taken, allergies, and known conditions. Apply it specifically within the “Medical scene safety and first helper role” procedure.

Use clean hands and clean barriers before touching wounds, body fluids, food, medicines, infants, or medical devices. Apply it specifically within the “Medical scene safety and first helper role” procedure.
Keep the person warm or cool as appropriate, still if trauma is possible, and protected from crowds. Apply it specifically within the “Medical scene safety and first helper role” procedure.
Recheck and record changes regularly so deterioration is noticed early. Apply it specifically within the “Medical scene safety and first helper role” procedure.

Prepare a short handover note with name, age, symptoms, times, medicines, allergies, and actions already taken. Apply it specifically within the “Medical scene safety and first helper role” procedure.
warnings: Do not use this as a replacement for training, emergency services, a clinician, or local medical guidance. Treat this as a specific boundary while managing “Medical scene safety and first helper role”.

Do not guess medicine doses, mix medicines without label checking, share prescriptions, or perform invasive procedures. Treat this as a specific boundary while managing “Medical scene safety and first helper role”.
Escalate urgently if you see: fire, traffic, electricity, gas smell, violence, unstable buildings, or more casualties.

Keep children, elders, pregnancy, disability, immune weakness, and chronic illness in the high-risk group. Treat this as a specific boundary while managing “Medical scene safety and first helper role”.
common_mistakes: Waiting too long after red flags appear while carrying out “Medical scene safety and first helper role”
Not writing times and medicines while carrying out “Medical scene safety and first helper role”
Touching wounds or fluids without hand hygiene while carrying out “Medical scene safety and first helper role”
Letting crowds block air, privacy, or transport while carrying out “Medical scene safety and first helper role”
Assuming a quiet person is stable without rechecking while carrying out “Medical scene safety and first helper role”
alternatives: Use official first-aid training when available as a fallback for “Medical scene safety and first helper role”
Use local emergency numbers and pharmacists for medicine questions as a fallback for “Medical scene safety and first helper role”
Use paper handover notes when phones are dead as a fallback for “Medical scene safety and first helper role”
Use a trusted translator if language blocks care as a fallback for “Medical scene safety and first helper role”
failure_signs: Red flags appear: fire, traffic, electricity, gas smell, violence, unstable buildings, or more casualties
The person becomes harder to wake, breathe, drink, speak, walk, or recognize during “Medical scene safety and first helper role”
Pain, bleeding, swelling, fever, confusion, or weakness gets worse during “Medical scene safety and first helper role”
when_not_to_use: Do not delay emergency care to complete the checklist when using the “Medical scene safety and first helper role” record
Do not move a trauma patient unless staying is more dangerous when using the “Medical scene safety and first helper role” record
Do not force care on a conscious person except immediate life safety situations under local rules when using the “Medical scene safety and first helper role” record
short_term: Stabilize the scene, reduce immediate harm, record key facts, and connect the person to trained help.
long_term: Keep medical records updated, train the group in certified first aid, maintain supplies, and practice respectful handovers.
if_method_fails: Escalate to emergency services, nearest clinic, pharmacist, radio medical consult, or evacuation route; document the failed step and time.
environment_notes: In apartments, heat, smoke, poor ventilation, crowds, no elevator, and water outages can make medical problems worse. Plan quiet space, clean water, light, and transport.

title: Unconscious person breathing watch
category: Medical and first · Aid · Disaster · Health
summary: Medical-heavy survival entry for watching breathing and protecting the person until help arrives. It is designed for offline emergencies and does not replace professional care.
content: Use this entry to organize watching breathing and protecting the person until help arrives. Start with scene safety, hand hygiene, calm communication, and written timing. Keep the person comfortable, protect privacy, avoid risky experiments, and prepare a clear handover for a doctor, ambulance, clinic, pharmacist, or trained first-aider. Watch especially for: not breathing normally, noisy gasping, blue lips, vomiting, seizure, or injury from a fall. In a long emergency, review the person regularly instead of assuming they are stable.
